explicit_text: During World War II, starting in 1939 when I was 17, the Germans invaded my village near Warsaw, Poland. I was taken hostage along with my brothers and separated from them to work on a farm in Germany. I stayed there until the war ended in 1945, experiencing both hardship and some kindness from the lady I worked for. The war completely changed my life and eventually led me to become a displaced person in Germany.

I was taken hostage along with my brothers and separated from them to work on a farm in the country that later became my place of displacement. I stayed there until the conflict ended in 1945, experiencing both hardship and some kindness from the lady I worked for. This war completely changed my life and eventually led me to become a displaced person in that country.

explicit_text: I grew up near Warsaw, Poland, and it was there that my life was turned upside down when the Germans came in 1939. My family and I were taken hostage in that region, which was the starting point of my difficult journey through the war and its aftermath. Later, I met my future wife Olga Shira, who also came from near Warsaw, so the city remained a meaningful connection for me even after I left.

explicit_text: I was born and raised in Poland, near Warsaw, and it was my homeland before the war disrupted everything. When the Germans invaded Poland in 1939, my family was taken hostage, and I was forced to work in Germany. Poland was the place I longed to return to, but after the war, I ended up in displaced persons camps in Germany before finally migrating to the United States.

explicit_text: After being taken hostage in Poland, I was sent to Germany to work on a farm for an older lady during the war. I stayed in Germany through the end of World War II in 1945 and then lived in a displaced persons camp there for about four years. The camp was near an American army base in what was called the American Zone in Germany. Germany was both a place of hardship and transition for me before I moved to the United States.
implicit_text: After being taken hostage in my home country, I was sent to the neighboring country to the west to work on a farm for an older lady during the war. I stayed there through the end of the global conflict in 1945 and then lived in a displaced persons camp there for about four years. The camp was near an American army base in what was called the American Zone in that western country. This place was both a location of hardship and transition for me before I moved to the United States.

explicit_text: After the war ended in 1945, I was classified as a displaced person and sent to a displaced persons camp in Germany. I stayed there for about four years, but I hated it because we were constantly moved and the food was terrible, especially during the hunger winter of 1948. Life in the camp was harsh, with limited calories and bland meals, and I even resorted to selling cigarettes on the black market to survive. It was a difficult chapter before I could finally emigrate to America.

explicit_text: The displaced persons camp where I stayed was located near an American army camp in the American Zone of Germany, likely in the southeast quadrant. My cousin John was stationed at that American army camp and helped me with the paperwork needed to move to the United States. The presence of the American Zone was a crucial factor in my eventual migration and new life abroad.

explicit_text: In 1949, I migrated to Bayonne, New Jersey, which is a suburb of New York. I moved in with my cousin Pete there, who encouraged me to learn English and helped me adjust to life in America. Bayonne was my first home in the United States and the place where I began building a new life after years of hardship in Europe.

explicit_text: After working various minor jobs for about a decade in New Jersey, I was hired in 1958 as an oil blender at the Fiske Bros. Oil Refinery in Newark. I worked there until my retirement in 1981. Newark became the place where I established my career and stability in the United States.
